questions.question: A teammate new to deep learning asks what the relationship between TensorFlow and Keras is in modern TensorFlow 2.x. Which answer is correct?
options: Keras (tf.keras) is the official high-level API bundled with TensorFlow for building and training models
Keras is a competing framework you must install separately and cannot mix with TensorFlow
TensorFlow is a plotting library and Keras does the math
Keras only works for inference, never training
questions.explanation: In TensorFlow 2.x, tf.keras is the official high-level API integrated into TensorFlow for defining, training, and evaluating models. It sits on top of TensorFlow's lower-level operations, so you use both together rather than choosing between them.

questions.question: You create a TensorFlow constant with `t = tf.constant([[1, 2], [3, 4]])`. You then try `t[0, 0] = 99` and it fails. Why?
options: The index is out of bounds
TensorFlow tensors cannot be 2-dimensional
tf.constant tensors are immutable; use a tf.Variable for values that must change
You must convert it to int64 first
questions.explanation: Tensors produced by tf.constant are immutable, so item assignment is not supported. State that changes during training (like weights) is held in tf.Variable, which supports in-place updates via methods like assign.

questions.question: Setting up a TensorFlow environment, you want training to use your GPU. Which check confirms TensorFlow can see the GPU?
options: `print(tf.__version__)` returns a number
The model compiles without error
`import numpy` succeeds
`tf.config.list_physical_devices('GPU')` returns a non-empty list
questions.explanation: `tf.config.list_physical_devices('GPU')` enumerates the GPUs TensorFlow has detected. A non-empty list means the GPU build and drivers are working; an empty list means TensorFlow will fall back to CPU regardless of having installed correctly.

questions.question: You add a NumPy array of shape (3, 1) to one of shape (1, 4) in a TensorFlow/NumPy workflow. Instead of an error you get a (3, 4) result. What rule produced this?
options: Matrix multiplication
Broadcasting, which stretches compatible dimensions to a common shape
Concatenation along axis 0
Element-wise comparison
questions.explanation: Broadcasting aligns shapes from the trailing dimension and stretches size-1 dimensions to match. A (3,1) and a (1,4) array broadcast to (3,4), applying the operation element-wise across the expanded grid without copying data explicitly.

questions.question: You decorate a Python function with `@tf.function`. What does this primarily change about how it executes?
options: It traces the function into a static computational graph for faster, optimized execution
It runs the function on the CPU only
It disables gradient computation permanently
It converts all tensors to NumPy arrays
questions.explanation: `@tf.function` traces the Python function into a TensorFlow graph (graph mode), enabling optimizations and faster repeated execution compared to eager mode. It does not pin execution to CPU or disable gradients; it changes eager calls into a compiled graph.

questions.question: Building your first model with the Keras Sequential API, when is the Sequential API the appropriate choice?
options: For models with multiple inputs and shared branches
Only for recurrent networks
For a plain stack of layers where each layer has exactly one input and one output tensor
For models that require skip connections
questions.explanation: The Sequential API expresses a simple linear stack where data flows through layers one after another, each with a single input and output. Models needing multiple inputs/outputs, branching, or skip connections require the Functional API instead.

questions.question: Your Dense output layer for a 10-class image classifier uses `activation='softmax'`. Which loss should you pair with it when labels are integer class indices?
options: binary_crossentropy
hinge
mean_squared_error
sparse_categorical_crossentropy
questions.explanation: Softmax outputs a probability distribution over classes. With integer (not one-hot) labels, sparse_categorical_crossentropy is the matching loss; categorical_crossentropy expects one-hot targets. MSE and binary crossentropy are inappropriate for multi-class classification.

questions.question: You choose the Adam optimizer over plain SGD for a deep network. What is the chief advantage Adam offers?
options: It guarantees the global minimum is reached
It adapts a per-parameter learning rate using estimates of first and second gradient moments
It eliminates the need for a loss function
It only updates the output layer
questions.explanation: Adam maintains running estimates of the mean (first moment) and uncentered variance (second moment) of gradients, adapting each parameter's effective learning rate. This typically yields faster, more stable convergence than vanilla SGD without manual LR tuning per parameter.

questions.question: After training, your model scores 99% on training data but 72% on a held-out test set. Why is keeping a separate test set essential here?
options: Only the test set gives an unbiased estimate of generalization, exposing the overfitting the training score hides
Test sets make training faster
Test sets are required by the optimizer
It increases the model's capacity
questions.explanation: A high train score with a much lower test score signals overfitting. The test set, untouched during training and tuning, provides an unbiased estimate of performance on unseen data, which the optimistic training metric cannot.

questions.question: In a Keras CNN, a `Conv2D(32, (3,3))` layer applied to a 28x28x1 input (no padding) produces a feature map of what spatial size, and why?
options: 28x28, because convolution preserves size by default
14x14, because convolution halves the dimensions
26x26, because a 3x3 valid convolution trims one pixel on each border
32x32, because there are 32 filters
questions.explanation: With 'valid' (no) padding, a 3x3 kernel cannot center on the outermost pixels, so each spatial dimension shrinks by kernel_size - 1 = 2, giving 26x26. The 32 filters set the output channel depth, not the spatial size.

questions.question: You add `MaxPooling2D(pool_size=(2,2))` after a convolution. What is its main purpose in the network?
options: To increase the number of channels
To convert the tensor into a probability distribution
To add trainable parameters for more capacity
To downsample feature maps, reducing spatial size and adding small translation invariance
questions.explanation: Max pooling takes the maximum over each window, halving spatial dimensions with a 2x2 pool. This reduces computation, enlarges the receptive field of later layers, and provides mild robustness to small translations. It has no trainable parameters.

questions.question: Adding a `BatchNormalization` layer between a Dense layer and its activation, what does batch normalization do during training?
options: It permanently fixes the weights of the previous layer
It normalizes each mini-batch's activations using batch mean/variance, then scales and shifts with learned parameters
It randomly drops half the activations
It increases the learning rate automatically
questions.explanation: Batch normalization standardizes activations using the current mini-batch statistics, then applies learnable gamma (scale) and beta (shift). This stabilizes and speeds training by reducing internal covariate shift; at inference it uses stored running statistics instead.

questions.question: You replace a unidirectional LSTM with a `Bidirectional(LSTM(...))` layer for sentiment classification. What does the bidirectional wrapper add?
options: It runs two LSTMs, one forward and one backward, so each timestep's representation sees both past and future context
It processes the sequence twice as fast on the same hardware
It removes the need for an embedding layer
It makes the model causal for real-time generation
questions.explanation: A bidirectional layer runs separate forward and backward passes over the sequence and combines them, letting each position incorporate context from both directions. This helps classification tasks, though it cannot be used for strictly causal real-time generation.

questions.question: Doing transfer learning with a pretrained Keras application (e.g. MobileNet), you set `base_model.trainable = False` and add a new classifier head. Why freeze the base initially?
options: To delete the pretrained weights
Because frozen layers train faster than unfrozen ones to higher accuracy
To preserve the learned general features while only the new head adapts to your classes, avoiding wrecking them with large early gradients
To convert the model to a regression model
questions.explanation: The pretrained convolutional base already encodes useful general features. Freezing it lets the randomly initialized head train without large gradients destroying those features; you may later unfreeze and fine-tune the base at a low learning rate.

questions.question: In the core skills application exercise you assemble a CNN: Conv2D and pooling blocks, then Flatten, then Dense layers to a softmax output. Why is the Flatten layer needed before the Dense layers?
options: Dense layers require a 4-D tensor input
Flatten performs the classification itself
Flatten adds trainable parameters that improve accuracy
Dense layers expect a 1-D feature vector per sample, so the 2-D feature maps must be flattened first
questions.explanation: Convolutional and pooling layers output multi-dimensional feature maps (height x width x channels), but a Dense layer operates on a flat feature vector per sample. Flatten reshapes the feature maps into a 1-D vector so the fully connected classifier can consume them; it has no trainable parameters.

questions.question: Comparing regularization choices, what distinguishes Dropout from L2 (weight decay) regularization?
options: They are identical and interchangeable
Dropout randomly zeroes activations during training to prevent co-adaptation; L2 penalizes large weights in the loss to keep them small
L2 only applies to convolutional layers and Dropout only to dense layers
Dropout increases weights while L2 decreases activations
questions.explanation: Dropout stochastically deactivates units during training so the network cannot rely on specific co-adapted neurons. L2 adds a penalty proportional to the squared weights, shrinking them toward zero. Both reduce overfitting but through different mechanisms.

questions.question: Training on CIFAR-10, you apply random flips and crops via a data augmentation pipeline. How does this improve the model?
options: It exposes the model to varied versions of each image, acting as regularization that improves generalization
It reduces the dataset size to speed training
It increases the number of output classes
It removes the need for a validation set
questions.explanation: Augmentation generates plausible transformed variants (flips, crops, shifts) of training images, effectively enlarging and diversifying the data. The model becomes invariant to these transformations and overfits less, improving test-time generalization.

questions.question: You add an `EarlyStopping` callback with `monitor='val_loss'` and `patience=5`. What behavior does this produce?
options: Training stops after exactly 5 epochs no matter what
It lowers the learning rate by 5x each epoch
Training stops if validation loss fails to improve for 5 consecutive epochs, preventing wasted epochs and overfitting
It saves the model every 5 batches
questions.explanation: EarlyStopping watches the monitored metric and halts training when it stops improving for `patience` epochs. With val_loss, this stops once validation performance plateaus or worsens, saving compute and curbing overfitting (optionally restoring best weights).

questions.question: Tuning hyperparameters via grid search, you evaluate each configuration using cross-validation rather than a single train/val split. What is the benefit?
options: It guarantees the chosen hyperparameters are globally optimal
It makes the search exhaustive over continuous ranges
It removes the need to train any models
It gives a more reliable performance estimate per configuration by averaging over multiple folds, reducing variance from one lucky split
questions.explanation: A single split can be lucky or unlucky, giving a noisy estimate. Cross-validation averages performance across several folds for each hyperparameter setting, yielding a lower-variance, more trustworthy comparison when selecting the best configuration.

questions.question: Your classifier reports 95% accuracy on a dataset that is 95% one class. The confusion matrix shows it almost never predicts the minority class. Which metrics best reveal this failure?
options: Accuracy alone, which already captures it
Precision, recall, and F1 (or per-class metrics), which expose poor minority-class performance
The training loss curve only
The number of epochs trained
questions.explanation: On imbalanced data, a majority-class predictor scores high accuracy while ignoring the minority class. Precision, recall, and F1 — especially for the minority class — and the confusion matrix reveal that the model fails on the cases that matter.

questions.question: Instead of a fixed learning rate, you schedule it to decay over epochs (e.g. via a LearningRateScheduler). Why can a decaying schedule help convergence?
options: Larger early steps explore quickly while smaller later steps allow fine settling near a minimum without overshooting
A constant LR always converges faster
It eliminates the need for an optimizer
Decay increases the batch size each epoch
questions.explanation: A higher learning rate early makes rapid progress across the loss surface, while gradually reducing it lets the optimizer take smaller, precise steps near a minimum, reducing oscillation and helping the model settle into a better solution.

questions.question: You need a model with two separate inputs (an image and a tabular vector) merged into one prediction. Which Keras API supports this, and the Sequential API cannot?
options: The Sequential API with an extra argument
A plain Python list of layers
The Functional API, which builds a graph of layers connecting multiple inputs and outputs
Only model subclassing can do it
questions.explanation: The Functional API treats layers as callable graph nodes, so you can define multiple Input tensors, branch, merge (e.g. concatenate), and produce multiple outputs. The Sequential API only models a single linear input-to-output stack.

questions.question: A multi-output model predicts both a category (classification) and a price (regression) from the same features. How do you typically configure its training?
options: Use a single loss for both outputs
Use only the regression loss and ignore the classification head
Train two completely separate models with no shared layers, always
Assign a separate loss to each output (e.g. crossentropy and MSE), optionally with loss weights, and Keras sums them
questions.explanation: Multi-output models attach an appropriate loss to each output — for example categorical crossentropy for the class head and MSE for the price head — combined (often weighted) into a total loss. Shared layers can still learn representations useful to both tasks.

questions.question: In a Keras attention/transformer block for NLP, what does the self-attention mechanism let each token's representation do?
options: Attend only to the immediately preceding token
Weigh and aggregate information from all other tokens in the sequence based on learned relevance
Ignore the rest of the sequence entirely
Convert the sequence into a fixed image
questions.explanation: Self-attention computes relevance (attention) weights between a token and every other token, then forms each token's new representation as a weighted sum over the sequence. This captures long-range dependencies that fixed-window convolutions or simple RNNs handle less directly.

questions.question: Preparing text for a Keras NLP model, you use a `TextVectorization` layer. What does it primarily do?
options: It standardizes, tokenizes, and maps text to integer sequences (and can be part of the saved model)
It trains the classifier head
It converts integers back into English sentences
It applies dropout to the embeddings
questions.explanation: TextVectorization handles standardization (e.g. lowercasing, stripping punctuation), tokenization, and mapping tokens to integer indices via a learned vocabulary. Building it into the model means raw strings can be fed at inference without a separate preprocessing step.

questions.question: In the advanced techniques exercise you build an NLP model whose first trainable layer is an Embedding. What does this Embedding layer learn to produce for each input token?
options: A fixed one-hot vector that never changes
The final softmax class probabilities
A trainable dense vector where semantically related tokens can end up close together
The optimizer's learning rate schedule
questions.explanation: An Embedding layer maps each integer token id to a trainable dense vector. Trained jointly with the task, these vectors position related tokens nearby in embedding space, giving downstream layers a richer, lower-dimensional representation than sparse one-hot encodings.

questions.question: Scaling training across multiple GPUs on one machine, you wrap model creation in a `tf.distribute.MirroredStrategy` scope. What does this strategy do?
options: It trains a different model on each GPU independently
It reduces the model to a single layer
It moves all computation to the CPU
It replicates the model on each GPU, splits each batch across them, and aggregates gradients to keep replicas in sync
questions.explanation: MirroredStrategy creates a mirrored copy of all variables on each GPU, splits each global batch across the replicas, and all-reduces gradients so every replica applies the same update. This delivers synchronous data-parallel training on multiple GPUs.

questions.question: Writing a custom training loop, you compute predictions and loss inside a `with tf.GradientTape() as tape:` block. Why must the forward pass be inside the tape's context?
options: The tape only formats the output for printing
The tape records the operations on watched variables so it can compute gradients of the loss with respect to them
The tape applies the optimizer automatically
Operations outside the tape run faster, so all code goes inside it
questions.explanation: GradientTape records operations involving trainable variables while inside its context. Only recorded operations can be differentiated, so the loss computation must occur within the tape; afterward `tape.gradient(loss, vars)` produces the gradients for the optimizer.

questions.question: Inside a custom training loop you call `tape.gradient(loss, model.trainable_variables)` and then must update the weights. Which call applies the update?
options: `optimizer.apply_gradients(zip(grads, model.trainable_variables))`
`model.fit(...)`
`loss.backward()`
`model.compile(...)`
questions.explanation: In a manual loop, after computing gradients with the tape you pass them and their variables to `optimizer.apply_gradients`, which performs the weight update. `model.fit` is the high-level alternative; `loss.backward()` is PyTorch syntax, not TensorFlow.

questions.question: Profiling a TensorFlow model with the TensorBoard profiler, you find the GPU is frequently idle while the input pipeline lags. Which tf.data technique most directly mitigates this?
options: Reduce the number of training epochs
Increase the model's depth
Add `.prefetch(tf.data.AUTOTUNE)` (and parallel map/caching) so data is prepared ahead of when the GPU needs it
Switch the optimizer to SGD
questions.explanation: An input-bound pipeline leaves the GPU waiting. `prefetch` overlaps data preparation with model execution so the next batch is ready in advance; parallelizing map and caching further reduces stalls. Model or optimizer changes do not fix an I/O bottleneck.

questions.question: In the end-to-end NLP capstone, you build an Embedding layer feeding an LSTM. What does the Embedding layer learn to provide?
options: A fixed one-hot vector per token that never changes
The optimizer's learning rate
The final class probabilities
A trainable dense vector representation for each token where semantically related tokens can end up nearby
questions.explanation: An Embedding layer maps each integer token id to a trainable dense vector. Trained with the task, these embeddings place related tokens near each other in vector space, giving the LSTM a richer, lower-dimensional input than sparse one-hot encodings.

questions.question: In TensorFlow 2.x, what is tf.keras?
options: The official high-level API bundled with TensorFlow for building and training models
A separate competing framework you cannot mix with TF
A plotting library
An inference-only tool
questions.explanation: tf.keras is TensorFlow's integrated high-level API for defining, training, and evaluating models, built on TensorFlow's lower-level ops. You use them together.

questions.question: `t = tf.constant([[1,2],[3,4]]); t[0,0] = 99` fails because:
options: The index is out of bounds
Tensors can't be 2-D
tf.constant tensors are immutable; use tf.Variable for mutable state
It must be int64 first
questions.explanation: Constants are immutable. Mutable, updatable state (like weights) lives in tf.Variable, which supports assign.

questions.question: Adding NumPy arrays of shape (3,1) and (1,4) yields a (3,4) result due to:
options: Matrix multiplication
Comparison
Concatenation
Broadcasting, which stretches size-1 dimensions to a common shape
questions.explanation: Broadcasting aligns trailing dimensions and stretches size-1 axes, so (3,1) and (1,4) expand to (3,4) for element-wise operations.

questions.question: Decorating a function with @tf.function primarily:
options: Pins it to CPU
Traces it into a static graph for optimized, faster execution
Disables gradients forever
Converts tensors to NumPy
questions.explanation: @tf.function compiles the Python function into a TensorFlow graph, enabling optimizations and faster repeated calls than eager mode.

questions.question: A softmax 10-class output with integer labels should use which loss?
options: sparse_categorical_crossentropy
binary_crossentropy
mean_squared_error
hinge
questions.explanation: Integer (non-one-hot) multi-class labels with softmax pair with sparse_categorical_crossentropy; categorical_crossentropy needs one-hot targets.

questions.question: Adam's main advantage over plain SGD is that it:
options: Guarantees the global minimum
Removes the loss function
Adapts a per-parameter learning rate from first/second gradient moments
Updates only the output layer
questions.explanation: Adam tracks running mean and variance of gradients to adapt each parameter's effective step size, usually giving faster, more stable convergence.

questions.question: A Conv2D(32,(3,3)) with valid padding on a 28x28x1 input outputs spatial size:
options: 26x26 (a 3x3 valid conv trims one pixel each border)
questions.explanation: Valid padding shrinks each dimension by kernel_size-1=2, giving 26x26; the 32 filters set channel depth, not spatial size.

questions.question: 99% train accuracy but 72% test accuracy means you need a test set because:
options: It speeds training
Only an untouched test set gives an unbiased generalization estimate, exposing overfitting
The optimizer requires it
It raises capacity
questions.explanation: The gap is overfitting. The held-out test set, unused in training/tuning, estimates true performance on unseen data, unlike the optimistic train score.

questions.question: BatchNormalization during training:
options: Normalizes each mini-batch's activations then scales/shifts with learned gamma and beta
Freezes prior-layer weights
Drops half the activations
Raises the learning rate
questions.explanation: BatchNorm standardizes activations by batch statistics and applies learnable scale/shift, stabilizing and speeding training; inference uses running stats.

questions.question: A Bidirectional(LSTM) wrapper adds the ability for each timestep to:
options: Train twice as fast
Skip the embedding layer
See both past and future context via forward and backward passes
Become causal for real-time generation
questions.explanation: It runs forward and backward LSTMs and combines them, so each position uses context from both directions — useful for classification, not causal generation.

questions.question: Dropout differs from L2 regularization in that Dropout:
options: Is identical to L2
Increases weights
Only applies to conv layers
Randomly zeroes activations during training to prevent co-adaptation, while L2 penalizes large weights
questions.explanation: Dropout stochastically deactivates units so the net can't rely on co-adapted neurons; L2 adds a squared-weight penalty shrinking weights. Both fight overfitting differently.

questions.question: Freezing a pretrained base (base_model.trainable=False) before training a new head:
options: Deletes the pretrained weights
Preserves learned general features while only the new head adapts, avoiding destruction by large early gradients
Always trains faster to higher accuracy
Makes it a regression model
questions.explanation: Freezing protects useful pretrained features while the random head trains; the base can later be unfrozen and fine-tuned at a low learning rate.

questions.question: Random flips and crops on CIFAR-10 improve the model by:
options: Exposing it to varied image versions, acting as regularization that improves generalization
Shrinking the dataset
Adding output classes
Removing the validation set
questions.explanation: Augmentation creates plausible variants, diversifying training data so the model becomes invariant to those transforms and overfits less.

questions.question: EarlyStopping(monitor='val_loss', patience=5):
options: Stops after exactly 5 epochs
Lowers the LR 5x per epoch
Stops when val_loss fails to improve for 5 consecutive epochs, curbing overfitting and waste
Saves every 5 batches
questions.explanation: It halts training when the monitored metric plateaus for patience epochs, saving compute and reducing overfitting (optionally restoring best weights).

questions.question: Using cross-validation per configuration in grid search helps because it:
options: Guarantees globally optimal params
Makes the search continuous
Removes the need to train
Averages performance over folds for a lower-variance, more reliable estimate than one split
questions.explanation: A single split is noisy; CV averages across folds for each setting, giving a more trustworthy comparison when picking the best configuration.

questions.question: 95% accuracy on a 95%-one-class dataset that never predicts the minority class is best diagnosed with:
options: Accuracy alone
Precision, recall, F1 (per-class) and the confusion matrix, which expose minority-class failure
Only the loss curve
The epoch count
questions.explanation: Accuracy is dominated by the majority class; precision/recall/F1 and the confusion matrix reveal the model fails on the minority class that matters.

questions.question: A model with an image input and a tabular input merged to one output requires which Keras API?
options: The Functional API, which builds a graph with multiple inputs/outputs
Sequential with an extra arg
A Python list of layers
Only subclassing
questions.explanation: The Functional API connects layers as a graph, supporting multiple Input tensors, branching, and merging — beyond the Sequential single-stack model.

questions.question: A multi-output model predicting a class and a price is trained by:
options: A single shared loss
Two fully separate models always
Assigning a separate loss per output (crossentropy and MSE), optionally weighted, summed by Keras
Ignoring the classification head
questions.explanation: Each output gets an appropriate loss, optionally weighted and summed; shared layers can still learn features useful to both heads.

questions.question: Self-attention lets each token's representation:
options: Attend only to the previous token
Become a fixed image
Ignore the sequence
Weigh and aggregate information from all tokens based on learned relevance
questions.explanation: Self-attention computes relevance weights across the whole sequence and forms each token's representation as a weighted sum, capturing long-range dependencies.

questions.question: Converting a Keras model to TensorFlow Lite is done mainly to:
options: Increase accuracy
Produce a small, optimized model for efficient on-device mobile/edge inference
Enable training
Make a spreadsheet
questions.explanation: TFLite yields a compact, often quantized model tuned for the limited memory and compute of phones and embedded devices, targeting efficient inference.

questions.question: Wrapping model creation in tf.distribute.MirroredStrategy scope:
options: Mirrors the model on each GPU, splits batches, and all-reduces gradients to keep replicas in sync
Trains a different model per GPU independently
Moves work to CPU
Reduces the model to one layer
questions.explanation: MirroredStrategy gives synchronous data-parallel training: mirrored variables per GPU, split batches, and gradient all-reduce so replicas stay identical.

questions.question: In a custom loop, the forward pass must be inside `with tf.GradientTape()` because the tape:
options: Only formats output
Applies the optimizer automatically
Records operations on watched variables so it can compute gradients of the loss w.r.t. them
Just speeds up code
questions.explanation: Only operations recorded within the tape's context can be differentiated; the loss must be computed there so tape.gradient can produce gradients.

questions.question: After computing grads with the tape, the call that updates weights is:
options: model.fit(...)
model.compile(...)
loss.backward()
optimizer.apply_gradients(zip(grads, model.trainable_variables))
questions.explanation: In a manual loop, optimizer.apply_gradients performs the update from the tape's gradients. loss.backward() is PyTorch; fit/compile are high-level Keras.

questions.question: An Embedding layer feeding an LSTM in the NLP capstone provides:
options: A fixed one-hot vector per token
A trainable dense vector per token where related tokens can end up nearby
The final class probabilities
The learning rate
questions.explanation: Embeddings map token ids to trainable dense vectors that, trained with the task, place related tokens near each other, giving the LSTM richer input than one-hot.
